pages.text: Fðr; sÞ¼ 0 ð1Þ For the interface failure condition—as a special case of Mohr’s theory—concrete is here, as it often is, assumed to be a Coulomb material. Furthermore, if the tensile strength is taken into account for the interface failure condition, a modiﬁed Coulomb material can be used ( Fig. 3, line B). Therefore, a repair or construction joint interface failure envelope can be modelled using three parameters: the tensile strength, f 0 t , the cohesion, c0, and the internal friction an- gle, /0. The term failure refers to cracking of the interface. Theoret- ically, there are three cracking failure modes: sliding, pure tension and a mixed mode. The sliding mode occurs when the shear strength limit is reached while the interface is under high compres- sive stress, whereas cracking under pure tension occurs with tensile stress but with zero shear stress. Between these two limiting condi- tions, e.g., in the case of low compressive stresses or when mainly under tension, shear failure occurs as a mixed mode of cracking. For a qualitative analysis of the resistance mechanisms of bond strength in interfaces between old and new concrete, we employed a 2D version of the graphical model for the evolution of a cracking envelope proposed by Carol [1] for normal/shear cracking in quasibrittle materials. This model was originally pro- posed to differentiate between ‘bond-adhesive’ and ‘bond-cohe- sive’ mechanisms. The ‘adhesive’ mechanism is related to the chemical forces act- ing at the micro-scale, which are responsible for the embedding ac- tion between the reactive matrix materials of the new concrete repair and the substrate of old repaired concrete. Xie and Xiong [2] presented a micromodel (see Fig. 2 b) based on the existence of a three-layer interface between the substrate and overlay. The ﬁrst layer, termed the ‘penetration layer’ (consisting mainly of prickly C–S–H with smaller amounts of AFt or Ca(OH) 2) is formed inside the concrete substrate and contains young constituents that Nomenclature f 0 t tensile strength of the interface, estimated from the splitting tensile test f 0 c;t compressive stress concomitant with the splitting ten- sile strength Pu ultimate or peak load registered during the splitting tensile test a width of the bearing strip used to distribute the load ap- plied to the cylinder B length of the cylinder used as a test specimen in the Bra- zilian test D diameter of the cylinder used as a test specimen in the Brazilian test y coordinate associated with a point located on the cylin- der radial plane, measured from the edge of the cylinder cov coefﬁcient of variation of the splitting tensile strength, deﬁned as the ratio of the standard deviation to the mean value / 0 internal friction angle of the interface material c0 cohesion of the interface material (parameter deﬁned for concrete plasticity corresponding to the pure shear resistance of the interface) rx transverse stress acting along the normal of the radial plane of the cylindrical splitting test specimen ry compressive stress parallel to the radial plane in the cylindrical splitting test specimen r1,i the highest-tension principal stress (e.g., in the splitting tensile test, this corresponds to the splitting tensile stress), located at i in Mohr’s circle r3,i the highest-compression principal stress (e.g., in slant shear tests, it corresponds to the uniaxial compression stress), located at i in Mohr’s circle r normal stress at the interface s shear stress at the

and highly oriented crystal constituents. The third layer, the ‘lower affected layer’, has almost the same micro-structure as the bulk new concrete. The ‘cohesive’ mechanism is linked to the ‘overlay transition zone’ (OTZ) of the new concrete and, therefore, is a material prop- erty. At the mesolevel, Beushausen and Alexander [3] reported that the interface failure occurs inside the overlay material. This inter- face failure is quite similar to the transition zone at the interface between aggregates and hardened cement paste (see Fig. 2c). Inter- face failure occurs in a highly porous material zone in the vicinity of the physical interface. The ‘interfacial transition zone’ (ITZ) in lightweight concrete with porous aggregates resembles the OTZ because the parent concrete behaves similarly to a porous aggre- gate surface. These two ‘bond-adhesive’ and ‘bond-cohesive’ mechanisms are closely associated. If the bond-adhesive strength is not developed, the interface cohesive mechanism will not be effective. The so-called ‘cohesion’ in Coulomb’s sliding expression repre- sents the overstrength that exists due to pure frictional behaviour. The ‘bond-cohesive’ mechanism is a further mechanism that acts in addition to the frictional one; it is shown graphically by the sepa- ration between curves (C) and (D) in Fig. 3. For this reason, once the bond-cohesion disappears, only the friction between aggregate particles remains as the shear resistance mechanism. In Fig. 3, Cou- lomb’s straight line (A) represents the ideal cracking envelope of a repaired cementitious interface. The tensile strength obtained with the Mohr–Coulomb sliding criterion has no physical meaning here, as the actual tensile strength is almost always smaller than this theoretical value, as shown by curve (B), which represents a mod- iﬁed Mohr–Coulomb envelope. In an attempt to ‘detach’ the interface bond resistance compo- nent, a hypothetical sequential degradation phenomenon is illus- trated in Fig. 3 . If the tensile strength of the interface is reached, the prime envelope evolves to an almost null tensile strength and an almost null cohesive (bond-adhesive) envelope (C). A fur- ther increase in external forces degrades the interface, causing damage to the cohesion (bond-cohesion) associated with the Cou- lomb sliding law (D). This stage probably occurs with shear crack- ing (inclined cracking path) surrounding a series of struts that transmit diagonal compression. The ﬁnal stage of damage (E) is reached when the friction angle of the interface diminishes and the interface zone behaves like a frictional soil enclosed by the adjacent unaffected concrete. 4. Analytical model for failure criteria As the proposed model is based on plasticity theory, a brief introductory discussion of the plasticity concepts concerning inter- faces between concrete bodies is given as follows. 4.1. Theoretical background The following concepts are based on the complete study of shear strength in joints—using the limit analysis and based on the plasticity theory of concrete—that was ﬁrst formulated by Jen- sen [4]. Let us consider a two concrete body composite prism with a plane interface between them forming an angle b with the nor- mal to the uniaxial compression, e.g., a typical specimen in the slant shear test. For the particular case in which the interface be- tween concrete bodies is perpendicular to the loading direction (i.e., b = 0 with no shear stress along the joint), the load carrying cap

This expression has a minimum at: bmin ¼ p 4 þ /0 2 ð4Þ Consequently, the compressive strength of the interface f 0 c (i.e., the minimum compressive strength of the composite prism) corre- sponds to an interface sliding failure, which is found by substitut- ing the value of bmin into expression (3): f 0 c ¼ 2c0 cos /0 1 /C0 sin /0 ð5Þ The dependence of the compressive strength on the angle b is shown in Fig. 5. Zone I corresponds to failure outside the concrete interface, and zone II corresponds to an interface sliding failure. Even so, when the slope of the interface is greater than b min, the failure of the interface depends on the tensile strength of the inter- face f 0 t . Assuming that the interface material law responds as in a modiﬁed Coulomb material, and based on the plasticity upper bound solutions, Jensen derived an equation that introduces the tensile strength of the interface to calculate the load carrying capacity of the joint between concretes. fc;b ¼ c0ð1 /C0 sin aÞ cos / þ f 0 t ðsin a /C0 sin /Þ cos b sinðb /C0 aÞð1 /C0 sin /Þ ð6Þ In formula (6), a is the angle of the relative displacement u be- tween both parts of the prism ﬂanking the interface. In the case of interfacial angles that are shallower than the critical angle, b 6 bmin and the normality condition (Von Misses ﬂow rule) holds: a = /0; hence, Eq. (6) becomes (3). However, if b > bmin, Eq. (3) is still valid but only up to the limiting angle blim. For the case in which f 0 t P 0, it can be demonstrated that this limiting angle is given by blim ¼ a tan ð1 /C0 sin /0Þ2 ð1 /C0 sin /0Þ cos /0 /C0 2k cos /0 "# þ /0 ð7Þ where k is the ratio between the tensile and compressive interfacial strengths k ¼ f 0 t =f 0 c . For the particular case in which f 0 t ¼ 0, the limit- ing angle equals the minimum angle blim = bmin. Therefore, the car- rying capacity will be constant for any angle greater than p/4 + /0/2 (represented by the horizontal line in Fig. 5 and by its correspond- ing circular curve o–q in Fig. 4 ). In the case of p=2 > b P bmin for f 0 t – 0, as a result of the convex form of the modiﬁed Coulomb failure envelope a P /; application of the Von Misses ﬂow rule to a circular transition curve yields the theoretical angle of displacement between concretes as a ¼ a sin sin b c0 /C0 f 0 t ðsin /0 þ cos /0Þ c0 /C0 f 0 t /C20/C21/C26/C27 þ b /C0 p 2 ð8Þ Likewise, the compressive principal stress f ’ c;t associated with the circular transition curve is f 0 c;t ¼ 2ðc0 /C0 f 0 t Þ ðsin /0 þ cos /0 /C0 1Þ /C0 f 0 t ð9Þ 4.2. Proposed failure envelope Using the tensile strength f 0 t , the friction angle /0 and the cohe- sion parameter c0, a smooth and continuous curve for the interface failure envelope can be deﬁned with Carol’s expression [1]: su ¼ ﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃ ðc0 /C0 r tan /0Þ2 /C0ð c0 /C0 f 0 t tan /0Þ2 q ð10Þ Since Eq. (10) represents a cracking envelope, once the failure condition (1) is reached, it seems logical to propose an evolution toward a ‘cracked’ interface. The evolution is considered as simply the process by which /0 remains constant, although the value of f 0 t falls to zero for normal interfacial stress r at low compression lev- els or under tensile stress. According to the concepts discussed in Section 4.1, the angle blim (7) can be understood as a threshold be- tween the frictional failure mode and the mixed failure mode. Con- sequently, the compressive stress threshold rlim related to blim can

Although statistical parameters can be used to objectively assess roughness quality, none of them yields a direct relationship with the friction angle /0 of the interface. The friction angles of Table 1 were derived from the graphical slope of a failure curve constructed mainly from slant shear prisms with different interfacial angles b (e.g., 60 /C176,7 0 /C176, etc.), although slant shear combined with direct shear testing was also used; note that the angle b must be greater than the angle bI,II (see Figs. 4 and 5). This table was compiled from experimental results reported in four prior studies [18,19,13,14,20]. A second table ( Table 2 ) was prepared with friction angles proposed by several authors as func- tions of a qualitative surface roughness evaluation. Based on the above compilation, the values proposed to qualitatively estimate the roughness of a concrete substrate are listed in Table 3 . 4.3.3. Cohesion parameter Desayi [21] proposed the determination of the failure of con- crete in a biaxial compression-tension stress system by simulta- neously applying tensile and compressive stresses produced by the splitting tensile test. Desayi used Wright’s expressions [22] (see Fig. 7 ), which consider the distributed load through the bear- ing strips (for a=d 6 0:10), so that lateral tensile stress rx as well as the vertical compressive stress ry can be assessed by rx ¼ 2Pu pBD 1 /C0 d 2a ða /C0 sin aÞ /C20/C21 ð15Þ ry ¼/C0 2Pu pBD d 2a ða þ sin aÞþ d d /C0 r /C0 1 /C20/C21 ð16Þ The main assumption of Desayi’s failure criterion is that the cracking causing the splitting failure extends over a considerable length of the loaded diameter. Conservatively, we propose the use of Circle 1 as the circular cut-off curve of the modiﬁed Mohr–Coulomb failure criterion for the concrete-to-concrete inter- face. For reasons of simplicity, this circle can be traced without appreciable error through the points r1,1 and r3,1, calculated respectively by Eqs. (13) and (14). Hence, analytically, the cohesion c0—as a function of the friction angle /0 and the splitting tensile stress f 0 t —can be obtained from Eq. (9), i.e.: c0 ¼ 2 /C0 sin /0 cos /0 /C18/C19 f 0 t for /0 > 30/C14 c0 ¼ ﬃﬃﬃ 3 p f 0 t for /0 6 30/C14 ð17Þ The cohesion values estimated through Eq. (17) are between 1.75 and 2.0 times the tensile strength of concrete-to-concrete cementitious interfaces (for /0 values varying between 37 /C176and 53/C176); this ratio of around two is in agreement with values found in the literature. Granju [23] reported that this ratio is between 2 and 3; Delatte et al. [24] measured a mean ratio of 2; Silfwerbrand [25] found a ratio between the torsion-shear bond strength and tensile pull-off strength in the range of 2–3; Robins and Austin [26,27] determined the experimental envelope with a pure shear strength to be approximately twice the magnitude of the direct tensile strength; and Yeoh [28] obtained a ratio of nearly 2 be- tween the results of core twist-off and pull-off tests. 5. Interface calibration tests Some investigators [26,29] have attempted to relate different interface tests with a failure criterion to generalise the bond strength concept for repaired concrete interfaces. Because calibra- tion of the failure envelope parameters requires experimental data obtained from interface calibration tests, a brief review of these tests is given in the following. Bond strength tests may be classiﬁed according to the method used to produce such interfacial stresses. There are three main groups: tension, ‘pure’ shear, and shear and compression based tests (Fig. 9). The ﬁrst group includes the pull-off test (a), the direct tension test (l) and the splitting tension test (m, n). The second group is broader and includes the torsion bond test (b), the direct shear test (c), the modiﬁed vertical shear bond test or compact shear test (f), the push-out test or push-through cube (j), the bi- surface shear test (k), and the guillotine test (g, h, i). The third group consists of the slant shear test (e) and the shear-compres- sion test (d). It is worth mentioning that the conditions in many of the tests denoted here as ‘pure’ shear tests (the second group in Fig. 9 ) are far from producing a theoretically pure shear state. This arises because this kind of shear test induces bending moment forces. Such forces can be readily visualised in the contours of the principal stresses when assessed by linear elastic ﬁnite element models. Emmons [30] reported that bond strength values obtained by the slant shear test or by the direct shear method are generally higher than direct tension results. Based on experimental interfa- cial tests, Júlio [31] presented a linear correlation between slant shear test and pull-off test results. Using an improved apparatus for torsion tests to measure interfacial shear stress, Silfwerbrand [25] showed that, with cast in place repair concrete, the ratio be- tween the average shear stress and the tensile stress varied be- tween 1.9 and 3.1, and with shotcrete, this ratio

For the compression and splitting tests, cylinders 150 mm in diameter and 300 mm long were used. The compressive failure mode of the ‘old’ concrete specimens was in the typical hourglass shape, indicating a fracture with a shear macro-cracking dissipa- tive mechanism. The new material used for the strengthening jack- et was a high strength, self-compacting micro-concrete (MH) made up of a commercially modiﬁed cementitious mortar (cement, a mix of sand type ﬁne aggregates, a superplasticiser admixture and polymers) and smooth rounded aggregates with a uniform grading curve. Table 6 shows the material proportions used to fabricate the micro-concrete, and the strength parameters are summarised in Table 5. The high ﬂuidity of these mortars was achieved by control- ling the oxide aggregate grading, using a high dosage of carboxyl- ate-type superﬂuid additives, and by using a high dosage of cement to obtain water–cement (w/c) ratios of about 0.35. It is remarkable that mortar manufacturers rarely explicitly offer this ratio, although they usually specify the mortar–water ratio, even directly printing it on the bags. 6.2. Testing program and specimen preparation The tests presented herein include seven specimens under split- ting tension and one under simple compression. All reported spec- imens were composite cylinders 150 mm in diameter and 300 mm long. The half cylinders of ‘old’ concrete ( Fig. 10 a) were obtained from the splitting tests. The substrate surface of the ‘old’ concrete half cylinders was a CSP around 9 (see Section 4.3.2). At the age of 586 days, the full cylinder was completed (i.e., the missing second half was restored) with fresh micro-concrete. The new concrete of the composite cylinder was cured in moulds covered with plastic bags for 5 days and then sealed with a plastic sheet for ageing be- fore testing. Splitting and compression tests of the composite specimens were performed 28 days after pouring the micro-concrete (616 days after the substrate concrete was cast) in a 5000 kN force-controlled AMSLER universal compressive testing machine (Fig. 10 b). The bearing platens were locked out against rotation to induce a uniform deformation along the boundary of the speci- men. The bearing strips used in the splitting tensile tests were made of laminated wood, 25 mm wide and 3 mm thick. Hence, the bearing-strip-to-cylinder-diameter ratio of the specimens was 1/6. Fig. 9. on the young material’s adhesion and substrate’s roughness. The micro-concrete tensile strength was the highest in these reported tests. However, in comparing the mean strength values, there was only an 11% difference between the micro-concrete strength and the most resistant old concrete (the difference was 64% when comparing compressive strength). 6.4. Observed interface failure The dominant, i.e., most common, interface failure mode was observed to occur within the OTZ. This type of interface failure was characterised by a thin layer of the overlay added to the old concrete substrate. Therefore, failure occurred principally in the micro-concrete material. Fig. 13 represents all of the mesofailure modes observed. Although the overall failure mode was within the OTZ (a), the ac- quired failure was actually a hybrid, since new concrete debonding was observed (b) (especially between the mortar matrix and old coarse aggregates), along with aggregate tensile failure (c), old coarse aggregate debonding (d), and material tensile failure in the overlay (e) and in the substrate (f). When the substrate surface was almost planar, the OTZ inter- face failure was very uniform and occurred throughout almost the whole interface; see Fig. 14 (left). However, when the substrate was irregular, especially when a wedge of new concrete ‘invaded’ part of the half cylinder of the old concrete, the failure mode was mixed and occurred in the new concrete, ﬁlling the wedge volume, as shown in Fig. 14 (right). Mixed failure modes were associated with high tensile bond strengths. This is due to the fact that it did not represent an actual measurement of the tensile strength of the interface, but rather a ‘‘mixed bond strength” between the interface (in the OTZ) and the new concrete material. 7. Validation of the proposed method To validate the proposed interface failure, which was mainly based on the splitting tensile test used to estimate the cohesion parameter, the Momayez test was employed. Momayez et al. [13,14] undertook an extensive test campaign (164 specimens) of pull-off, splitting prism, slant shear and direct shear tests. The di- rect shear tests, termed ‘bi-surface’ tests by these authors, were conducted on cubic specimens in which the repair material consti- tuted one-third of the specimen. The old concrete substrate was roughened to two different degrees: to an estimated amplitude of 3–4 mm (L) and to 7–8 mm (H). Since the failure envelope proposed in this work is only valid for cementitious interfaces (without foreign agents), only the modiﬁed cement based materials tested in both studies were used for com- parison. The sand cement mortars used for repair were coded with respect to their silica fume contents. The code R was used for a ref- erence (0% silica fume) and the numeral codes 5, 7, and 10 for 5%, 7% and 10% were used to designate silica fume contents, respectively. To construct the failure envelopes, the tensile strength parame- ter was calculated as the average value between the pull-off and splitting tensile test results. Cohesion was predicted using Eq. (17), and the angle of friction was calculated from a trigonometric relation between the points of the direct shear and slant shear test Fig. 13. Estimation of bond strength envelopes for old-to-new concrete interfaces based on a cylinder splitting test Ariel D. Espeche a,b,⇑, Javier León a,c a Dept. of Continuum Mechanics and Structures, Group of Structural Concrete, Polytechnic University of Madrid, Madrid, Spain b Advanced Design and Analysis Division, IDOM, Madrid, Spain c FHECOR Consulting Engineers, Madrid, Spain article info Article history: Received 1 December 2009 Received in revised form 4 August 2010 Accepted 2 September 2010 Available online 28 September 2010 Keywords: Bond strength Interface failure envelope Rehabilitation Overlay Patching repairs abstract The number of existing structures under rehabilitation has signiﬁcantly increased over the past two dec- ades; these structures typically require performance improvements including repair and strengthening. Currently, reinforced concrete is widely used in interventions for the rehabilitation of bridges and build- ings. Here, we present a method for the estimation of failure envelopes for old-to-new concrete interfaces based on plasticity theory. The proposed Carol-type failure envelope uses the simple splitting tension test, also known as the Brazilian test. The relevant parameters of the envelope— f 0 t , the tensile strength, c0, cohesion and /0, the friction angle—are assessed by experimentation, a rational formulation and a bib- liographic study, respectively. /C2112010 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction The ageing of existing concrete structures demands work in maintenance, repair and strengthening measures. Cementitious materials, either mortar or concrete, are widely used in the rehabil- itation of bridges and buildings; a common practice involves the addition of a thin concrete layer, ranging from 20 to 100 mm, either alone or reinforced with steel bars or metallic or synthetic ﬁbres. The patching or substitution of concrete overlay in ﬂoors or decks and the use of structural concrete jacketing columns are two common exam- ples of the application of cementitious materials in strengthening. The aim of this study was to provide a criterion for evaluating the bond strength between newly added and existing concrete in such applications. For this purpose, we developed a simple method to estimate the bond strength of the interface between old and new concrete using a failure envelope based on the theory of plas- ticity. An experimental campaign using the splitting tensile test was developed to assess the tensile bond strength between old concrete and a micro-concrete repair. 2. Examples of shear stresses at interfaces There are some typical stress states at interfaces that must be considered in assessing the suitability of a failure envelope. The interface between the substrate concrete of existing slabs and newly poured concrete is subject to a self-equilibrated state of ten- sion combined with shear ( Fig. 1-I). These stresses, which appear in the serviceability state, are mainly located at the borders and are caused by restrained drying shrinkage. Bridge retroﬁtting work is sometimes necessary in repairing and strengthening slender stems of box girder decks, generally due to a lack of shear reinforcement (Fig. 1 -II A). In these cases, the interfaces between new and old concrete are under shear stress due to variations in the overall bending moment once the new dead load and the live load act to- gether. Columns strengthened by means of high-performance con- crete jackets have a transference zo
